new_calls.description: A low, rough growl from the hen wigeon — often heard on marshes.
new_calls.proTips: Guttural and low. Very different from a mallard quack — more like a short growl.

new_calls.description: The iconic three-note 'whew-whew-whew' whistle of the drake wigeon.
new_calls.proTips: Three breathy whistles, rising slightly. One of the easiest duck calls to learn.

new_calls.description: A quick, squeaky alarm given before diving or taking flight.
new_calls.proTips: Short squeaky bursts. Buffleheads prefer diving to flying — mirror that nervousness.

new_calls.description: The hurried chattering a hen bufflehead makes when flushed from cover.
new_calls.proTips: Rapid, nasal chattering. Buffleheads are quiet ducks — this is their loudest moment.

new_calls.description: A longer social call used between paired birds on calm water.
new_calls.proTips: Soft and nasal. Buffleheads are tiny — keep the volume down.

new_calls.description: A soft clucking sound blue-wings make while loafing on water.
new_calls.proTips: Gentle, quiet. Blue-wings are often silent — this is their casual chatter.

new_calls.description: A thin, lisping flight call blue-wings give when moving between feeding areas.
new_calls.proTips: Thin and high. Blue-wings are small and their calls reflect that.

new_calls.description: A complex vocalization the drake produces during breeding display.
new_calls.proTips: Multi-note, musical. This is the showpiece call of the blue-wing drake.

new_calls.description: The urgent, rapid cawing crows give when a hawk or owl is spotted.
new_calls.proTips: Fast, aggressive caws. This is the 'DANGER!' call — sell the panic.

new_calls.description: A sustained cawing sequence — the standard 'I'm here' territorial call.
new_calls.proTips: Steady, rhythmic caws. Each one should be clean and equally spaced.

new_calls.description: A clear, high-pitched 'pe-chee' whistle given in flight and on the ground.
new_calls.proTips: Clean two-note whistle, rising sharply. Very different from dabbling duck calls.

new_calls.description: A nasal, reedy alarm call — the gadwall's signature nervous quack.
new_calls.proTips: Nasally and reedy, not as loud as a mallard. Think 'meh' with attitude.

new_calls.description: A sustained alarm calling sequence, more urgent than the single alarm.
new_calls.proTips: Faster tempo than the single alarm. Escalating urgency.

new_calls.description: The subtle flight call male gadwalls make — a short, nasal 'nheck.'
new_calls.proTips: Very understated. Gadwalls are the 'gray ghost' — their calls match their subtlety.

new_calls.description: A sharp, high-pitched peeping alarm that green-wings give when startled.
new_calls.proTips: Quick, nervous bursts. These tiny ducks are skittish — sell the urgency.

new_calls.description: The distinctive high whistle given by drakes during fast, agile flight.
new_calls.proTips: Thin, reedy whistle. Green-wings are the fighter jets of ducks — match that speed.

new_calls.description: A steady, repetitive peeping used for social contact on the water.
new_calls.proTips: Even rhythm, consistent pitch. Think 'I'm here, I'm here, I'm here.'

new_calls.description: The crisp, ringing whistle of the drake — a signature green-wing sound.
new_calls.proTips: Clean, clear whistle with a slight upward inflection at the end.

new_calls.description: A raw, piercing scream — used for long-distance territorial communication.
new_calls.proTips: Deep, powerful sound from the chest. Feel the vibration in your core.

new_calls.description: A sharp, rhythmic series of quacks given by hens while in flight to maintain flock contact.
new_calls.proTips: Keep notes quick and crisp. Slightly higher pitch than resting calls.

new_calls.description: A continuous calling pattern used during sustained flight, signaling to other ducks below.
new_calls.proTips: Maintain a steady rhythm without pausing between quacks.

new_calls.description: An extended sequence of decreasing quacks, the classic highball to lonesome hen pattern.
new_calls.proTips: Start loud and gradually decrease volume. Each quack should be slightly shorter than the last.

new_calls.description: The tail end of a long calling sequence — soft, pleading quacks that seal the deal.
new_calls.proTips: Soft and subtle. This is the 'I'm here, where are you?' part of the sequence.

new_calls.description: A quack punctuated by a high chirp — a contented feeding sound that reassures nearby birds.
new_calls.proTips: Mix quack with a quick upward chirp. Mimics a hen feeding confidently.

new_calls.description: A rapid burst of quacks used to grab attention, effective for close-range decoying.
new_calls.proTips: Short, punchy quacks — think 'come here NOW.' Don't overdo the volume.

new_calls.description: A low, breathy hissing quack — muscovies are nearly silent compared to other ducks.
new_calls.proTips: Very quiet and breathy. Muscovies rarely vocalize loudly — keep it subdued.

new_calls.description: A low, nasal 'took-took' alarm call unique to shovelers.
new_calls.proTips: Low and hollow-sounding. The oversized bill creates a unique resonance.

new_calls.description: A soft chirping contact sound shovelers make while feeding in groups.
new_calls.proTips: Soft, bubbly chirps. Think of it as 'contented background noise.'

new_calls.description: A rapid call burst used as an attention-getter on the water.
new_calls.proTips: Quick and nasally. Shovelers tend to call in short bursts.

new_calls.description: The soft tree yelps turkeys make at dusk as they settle onto roost branches.
new_calls.proTips: Soft, muffled yelps. Roosting turkeys are calm — match that mood.

new_calls.description: A distinctive vocalization not commonly heard — effective for curious toms.
new_calls.proTips: Unusual cadence that stands out. Use sparingly for maximum effect.

new_calls.description: The panicked 'oo-eek!' a hen wood duck gives when flushed from cover.
new_calls.proTips: Rising, squealing note. Very distinctive — like a surprised squeak.

new_calls.description: The thin whistle wood ducks make during flight through wooded corridors.
new_calls.proTips: Thin, wheezy whistle. Wood ducks fly through tight spaces — light, agile sound.

new_calls.description: A loud, harsh alarm sound given when a wood duck senses danger.
new_calls.proTips: Loud and sharp — the woodie is genuinely afraid. Maximum urgency.

new_calls.description: The classic ascending squeal — the most recognizable wood duck sound.
new_calls.proTips: Rising squeal that drops off. This IS the wood duck sound everyone knows.
